title: SEGMENT 16 — Ethics: religion, animal welfare, and informed consent

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Ethics: religion, animal welfare, and informed consent
Every time I give this lecture, somebody raises their hand and asks about ethics.
So let me address the question head-on.
There are three layers of ethical concern, and they are not the same.
Religious considerations.
Animal welfare.
Informed consent.
Let me take them in turn.
First, religion.
Several major religious traditions — particularly Islam and Judaism — have dietary prohibitions on pork.
The question that people often ask is: does a Muslim or Jewish patient face a religious problem in receiving a pig organ?

The short answer, which I want to be careful with because I am not a religious scholar and the views vary, is that the major religious authorities in both traditions have generally ruled that life-saving medical use of pig tissue is permitted.
The principle invoked is, broadly, that the prohibition on pork is dietary — it relates to consuming pig flesh as food — and that medical use to save a life falls under a separate framework.
In Islamic jurisprudence, the principle is sometimes called darura, necessity, which permits otherwise-prohibited acts when life is at stake.
In Jewish law, the principle is pikuach nefesh, the supreme value of preserving life.
Most rabbinical and Islamic medical-ethics opinions have, in fact, ruled that pig organ transplants are permissible.
Individual patients of course consult with their own religious advisors and make their own decisions, and not every authority agrees, but the major-tradition position is permissive.

Hindu and Buddhist traditions, which take more general views on animal welfare and non-violence, also tend to permit pig transplants on similar life-saving grounds, though again with individual variation.
Second, animal welfare.
The pigs in question are bred specifically to be donors.
They live in sterile facilities, are anesthetized at the time of organ procurement, and are euthanized in the process.
There is a meaningful animal-welfare cost to xeno — one pig dies for each transplanted organ.
Compared to the hundred and thirty million pigs killed annually for food in the United States, the marginal animal-welfare cost is small.
But it is not zero.
Some ethicists have argued that we should be raising xeno pigs in higher-welfare conditions than commercial farm pigs, even if the lifespan is short.
Some have argued the opposite, that the use of an animal to save a human life is straightforwardly justified.
The debate is active.
My own view is that the welfare standards applied to xeno pigs are, in general, higher than those applied to commercial swine, and that this is appropriate.
Third, and most subtle, informed consent.
The xeno patients we've talked about — Bennett, Slayman, Pisano, Looney — were all patients with no good alternative.
The compassionate use pathway, by design, restricts xenotransplantation to patients who would otherwise die.
The question is whether such patients can genuinely give informed consent when the alternative is death.
Critics argue that the desperate cannot freely consent.
Defenders argue that the alternative — refusing to offer experimental therapies to the dying — is itself a moral wrong, depriving them of agency.

The field's response, in practice, has been to invest heavily in the consent process — extended conversations, family involvement, independent ethics review boards, the option to decline at any point — to make consent as informed as it can be.
Whether that is enough is, I think, a judgment each of you will have to make.
There is also the public health consent question.
Xenotransplant patients are agreeing, on behalf of the rest of us, to be possible vectors for novel zoonotic disease.
The surveillance and contact restrictions are partial mitigations.
But the patients are accepting risk on behalf of a society that is, in effect, conducting a long-term experiment through them.
This is an ethical structure we have used for clinical trials for a long time, but it is more vivid in xenotransplant than in most other settings.
I don't have a tidy answer here.
I think it's a real consideration.
I think it has to be balanced against the very real loss of life on the waiting list.
